items.headline: CRISPR-Engineered Stem Cells for Parkinson’s Therapy
items.preview: Researchers have developed CRISPR-Cas9 engineered stem cells aimed at treating Parkinson's disease.
items.article:
In a groundbreaking advancement that could redefine therapeutic strategies for neurodegenerative disorders, researchers have harnessed the precision of CRISPR–Cas9 gene editing technology to engineer human pluripotent stem cells with unparalleled specificity aimed at combating Parkinson’s disease. This study leverages the transformative potential of pluripotent stem cells and combines this with the surgical precision of CRISPR–Cas9, breathing new life into hopes for curative approaches.
Why this matters: The integration of CRISPR–Cas9 gene editing with human pluripotent stem cell technology represents a paradigm shift in Parkinson’s disease research and therapy, promising a new era of precision regenerative neurology.

items.headline: Top biotech deals in March 2026
items.preview: March 2026 saw a surge in biotech mergers and acquisitions, surpassing licensing deals.
items.article:
Biotech deal activity seemed to be thriving in March 2026, with the number of mergers and acquisitions (M&As) beating licensing deals. The final month of the first quarter witnessed the highest number of M&As, with 12 major deals announced, including Lilly’s acquisition of Centessa Pharmaceuticals for $7.8 billion. This trend highlights a robust market for biotech investments and strategic consolidations.
Why this matters: The surge in M&A activity indicates a strong confidence in the biotech sector, potentially leading to accelerated innovation and development of new therapies.

items.headline: Female Sexual Dysfunction After Anal Cancer Radiotherapy
items.preview: A systematic review highlights the prevalence of sexual dysfunction in women post-anal cancer treatment.
items.article:
In a groundbreaking systematic review published in the British Journal of Cancer, researchers have delved into a largely overlooked consequence of anal cancer treatment: female sexual dysfunction following radiotherapy. The findings underscore an urgent need for integrated multidisciplinary care that addresses quality of life alongside cancer eradication.
Why this matters: This review advocates for the establishment of clinical pathways that systematically screen and manage sexual dysfunction in female anal cancer survivors, emphasizing the importance of holistic care.

items.headline: Structural insights into subtype-specific agonist recognition by sphingosine-1-phosphate receptors
items.preview: New research reveals how agonists selectively bind to different sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor subtypes.
items.article:
This study reports four cryo-electron microscopy structures of agonist-bound S1PR1-Gi1 complexes, uncovering the molecular basis for the selectivity of various agonists toward S1PR1 over other subtypes. Understanding these interactions is crucial for the rational design of targeted therapeutic agents.
Why this matters: These findings provide a foundation for developing highly selective S1PR1 modulators, which could lead to improved therapeutic options for conditions influenced by these receptors.

items.headline: How long-read sequencing is scaling beyond the specialist lab
items.preview: Advancements in long-read sequencing technology are making it more accessible for large-scale research.
items.article:
Advances in genomic sequencing have led to significant gains in our understanding of biology. Long-read sequencing is now becoming accessible at scale as throughput increases and costs decline. This technology allows for a more complete view of genomes, capturing complex structural variations that short-read sequencing misses.
Why this matters: The scalability of long-read sequencing is expected to drive breakthroughs in population genomics, rare diseases, and cancer research, enhancing our ability to understand and treat various conditions.
